coredata.dc:description: This study developed a gelatin/alginate-based nanocomposite hydrogel (NC gel), incorporating selenium-doped deferoxamine-derived carbon quantum dots (Se.DFO-CQDs). Initially, Se.DFO-CQDs were synthesized and characterized through several tests, and subsequently, NC gels were created using an dual crosslinking method and analyzed through characterization tests such as SEM, EDX, FT-IR, XRD, tensile strength, water uptake, water vapor transmission rate, weight loss, porosity, blood compatibility, microbial penetration, and DPPH. In vivo studies revealed that NC gels containing Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % and 0 % exhibited higher wound closure percentages than the control group. The highest wound closure percentage was observed in NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 %, reaching 85.7 ± 3.98 % on the 7th day and 98.1 ± 3.95 % on the 14th day. Histological examinations demonstrated that NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % promoted more significant neovascularization, re-epithelialization, and collagen synthesis. Additionally, RT-qPCR results indicated that NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % significantly upregulated the mRNA expression of VEGF-A, bFGF, PDGF-b, and lncRNA GAS5 on the 7th day and COL1A1 on the 14th day. In conclusion, our findings suggest that the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % show promise for enhancing wound healing and skin regeneration, potentially offering clinical applications.

head.ce:title: Fabrication, characterization, and application of gelatin/alginate-based hydrogels incorporating selenium-doped deferoxamine-derived carbon quantum dots: In vitro and in vivo studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study developed a gelatin/alginate-based nanocomposite hydrogel (NC gel), incorporating selenium-doped deferoxamine-derived carbon quantum dots (Se.DFO-CQDs). Initially, Se.DFO-CQDs were synthesized and characterized through several tests, and subsequently, NC gels were created using an dual crosslinking method and analyzed through characterization tests such as SEM, EDX, FT-IR, XRD, tensile strength, water uptake, water vapor transmission rate, weight loss, porosity, blood compatibility, microbial penetration, and DPPH. In vivo studies revealed that NC gels containing Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % and 0 % exhibited higher wound closure percentages than the control group. The highest wound closure percentage was observed in NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 %, reaching 85.7 ± 3.98 % on the 7th day and 98.1 ± 3.95 % on the 14th day. Histological examinations demonstrated that NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % promoted more significant neovascularization, re-epithelialization, and collagen synthesis. Additionally, RT-qPCR results indicated that NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % significantly upregulated the mRNA expression of VEGF-A, bFGF, PDGF-b, and lncRNA GAS5 on the 7th day and COL1A1 on the 14th day. In conclusion, our findings suggest that the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % show promise for enhancing wound healing and skin regeneration, potentially offering clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The skin is the largest organ of the integumentary system, which covers the outer part of the body. There is no doubt that various pathophysiological conditions or trauma can damage this organ and cause several healthcare problems, such as infection [1,2]. The wound healing process comprises several phases, including homeostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling [3]. In these four phases, various interactions exist between different cells and factors, ultimately leading to the repair of the damaged area [4].

Numerous growth factors and peptides are involved in the initiation and progression of wound healing [5]. Among the most important growth factors, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF), and platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) have crucial roles in skin repair [5–7]. Collagens are the most numerous proteins in mammals, constituting about 30 % of the total protein mass [8]. Collagen type I alpha 1 (COL1A1) is one of the most abundant collagens, ubiquitously expressed across tissues. Several studies have stated that COL1A1 is involved in wound repair through neovascularization, infiltration of endothelial progenitor and immune cells, cell migration, re-epithelialization, and remodeling [8].

Another crucial group of molecules involved in the pathogenesis of many diseases are long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs), which have >200 nucleotides and no coding potential. Many lncRNAs have key roles in the regulation of lymphangiogenesis [9]. The lncRNA growth-arrest specific 5 (GAS5) is one of the main lncRNAs with tumor suppressor gene features. Previous studies have shown that lncRNA GAS5 can activate VEGF through binding to TATA box-binding protein associated factor 15 (TAF15), leading to accelerated wound healing [10].

Significant advances have been made in chemistry, physics, biology, tissue engineering, and wound healing using nanotechnology, leading to the development of various types of wound dressings, such as nanofibers, hydrocolloids, nanocomposite hydrogels (NC gels) to re-epithelialize the native functional skin [11,12]. NC gels comprise of a three-dimensional polymer network with large amounts of water, exhibiting different and interesting swelling/expansion, optical, and mechanical properties used in drug delivery and wound healing [13,14]. Gelatin (gel) and alginate (alg) are the most important polymers used in hydrogel wound dressings due to their features such as massive liquid absorbing capacity, non-immunogenicity, biodegradability, and excellent biocompatibility [15].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or fluorescent carbon nanoparticles are a novel group of carbon-based nanomaterials with excellent properties like low toxicity, great biocompatibility, low cost, chemical inertness, ease of preparation, simple synthetic routes, resistance to photobleaching, and environmental friendliness. CQDs have critical applications in nanomedicine, bioimaging, chemical sensing, biosensing, electrocatalysis, and photocatalysis [16,17]. Studies have shown that the connection of metal ions to CQDs can change their basic properties, creating metal-doped-CQDs (M-CQDs) with excellent chemical and biological features [16].

Deferoxamine mesylate or desferrioxamine (DFO) is a small molecule drug approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), known for its chelating effect via binding to iron, leading to suppress oxidative stress by blocking the Fenton and Haber-Weiss reactions, and also improving wound healing through increasing neovascularization. DFO can be used topically in various forms to accelerate the wound-healing process [18–20].

Selenium (Se) is an essential trace element in the polypeptide chain as part of the selenocysteine (Sec) amino acid. Proteins containing Sec are known as selenoproteins, showing outstanding antioxidant activity and anti-inflammatory features [21]. Several studies have shown that selenium can be involved in wound healing, particularly in people with diabetes, by enhancing angiogenesis, decreasing lipid peroxidation and free radicals, and accelerating wound healing [22].

Although extensive experiments have been conducted in the field of M-CQDs and wound healing, limitations still need to be made on how to apply and use different medicines and elements like M-CQDs effectively in the wound healing process. DFO has shown efficiency in wound repair, with various studies demonstrating its effectiveness in different forms [23–26]. However, DFO has not been synthesized as M-CQDs and incorporated into NC gel as a wound dressing mat. This study addresses this limitation by using DFO and Se as M-CQDs and applying them as a hydrogel wound dressing to have better effects and avoid the consequences of skin damage in various diseases and trauma.

The novelty of this research lies in the synthesis of gelatin/alginate-based hydrogels incorporating selenium-doped deferoxamine-derived carbon quantum dots (Se.DFO-CQDs). Firstly, Se.DFO-CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal approach, followed by various characterization analyses. Secondly, an NC gel was made using an idual crosslinking method that included gelatin/alginate as polymers, serving as a carrier for Se.DFO-CQDs, numerous characterization tests were conducted for the NC gel. Thirdly, the properties of the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs were evaluated using in vitro techniques to determine the appropriate concentration for skin wound dressing. Finally, the adaptable concentration was used as the final formulation in the wound dressing in a rat model of full-thickness excisional wounds, and the effects of this wound dressing on skin repair were investigated through histopathology and molecular evaluations.

To prepare Se.DFOs-CQDs, firstly, 50 mg deferoxamine (Afa Chemi Pharm. Co. Tehran, Iran) was dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water (DW) using a stirrer. Then, 5 mg selenium dioxide (Merck, Germany) was added to the prepared solution. The solution was transferred into the thermal reactor and placed into an oven at 250 C0 for 4 h to synthesize the Se.DFO-CQDs using the hydrothermal method. To separate the larger particles, centrifugation was performed for 10 min at 12000 rpm; then, the supernatant solution was separated. On the other hand, a 0.22 μm dialysis bag was engaged to separate the smaller particles. The dialysis was done several times.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Se.DFO-CQDs were obtained by TEM (Philips EM 208S 100 KV, Netherlands) [27]. UV–Vis spectra of Se.DFO-CQDs were obtained from 250 to 500 nm by spectrophotometer (spectrumlab-53, Iran) [28]. The fluorescent spectra were recorded to determine the excitation and emission features of Se.DFO-CQDs on a spectrofluorometer (FP-6200, Japan). The size and the surface charge of DFO-CQDs and Se.DFO-CQDs were measured using the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique (Nanoparticle analyzer SZ-100 V2, Japan). Zeta potential of DFO-CQDs and Se.DFO-CQDs were measured by a Nanoparticle analyzer (SZ-100 V2, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained to determine Se's physical condition of Se.DFO-CQDs using a powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, AXS D8 Advance, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded to indicate the repetitions of vibrations of bonds existent atoms of Se, DFO, and Se.DFO-CQDs on an FT-IR Spectrometer (Spectrum 65, USA). (N:3).

In vitro antioxidant study of Se.DFO-CQDs with different percentages (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, and 100) were done as per standard 1, 1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, USA) assay [29]. In practice, 1000 μL of 1 mM DPPH was mixed with 300 μL of samples (Se.DFO-CQDs) and were incubated at 37 C0 for 3 h. The absorption of the samples was examined at 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210, and 240 min at 517 nm. Absolute ethanol was used as the control. The radical scavenging activity was calculated using the following eq. 1; (N:3)
(1)
DPPH scavenging
%
=
Absorbance Control – Absorbance Sample
Absorbance Control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanocomposite gel/Alg hydrogels (NC gels)
ce:section.ce:para: The dual crosslinking method synthesized the NC gels by mutual interaction between polymer anionic chains and divalent cations. For this purpose, gelatin and sodium alginate (Merk, Germany) polymer solution with different percentages of Gel/Alg (Gel 7 % / Alg 3 %), (Gel 8 % / Alg 2 %), and (Gel 9 % / Alg 1 %) was dissolved in DW. The polymers were gently mixed by a magnet at 40 C0 for 1 h until a homogeneous solution was obtained. Then, glyoxal/calcium chloride (Merk, Germany) 100 mM solution was used as a dual cross-linker and added to the Gel/Alg polymer solution. The solution containing Gel/Alg (polymers) and glyoxal/calcium chloride (cross-linker) was mixed slowly for 10 min; after 24 h, the hydrogel network was formed (Figs. S1a and b). Three different percentages of hydrogel were prepared and the releasing Se.DFO-CQDs were evaluated. The Gel 8 % / Alg 2 % hydrogel had the most suitable release of Se.DFO-CQDs were used in the following stages of the research.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging was carried out to evaluate the morphology of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 %. The NC gels were completely dried by freeze dryer (Operon, FDB-5503, Italy) and prepared for SEM and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. SEM images were obtained by SEM FEI (Model Quanta 450 FEG, Hillsboro, OR, USA). EDX analysis is a method for elemental detection that collects characteristic X-rays induced via electron beam radiation. In this study, the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 %, and 100 % were prepared for EDX analysis (EDAX-EDS analyzer, TESCAN MIRA2, the Czech Republic). (N:3).
XRD patterns of Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, and 100 % were obtained using a powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, AXS D8 Advance, Germany), like Se.DFO-CQDs. (N:3).

Mechanical properties, like tensile strength, are the important characteristics that determine the applicability of wound dressing. The NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 %, and 100 % were used for tensile forces; more precisely, the NC gels were prepared in a rectangle with 5 mm thickness, 70 mm length, and 10 mm width. 10 mm/min strain rate and 10-N force by a universal mechanical testing machine (STM-20, SANTAM Experimental Instrument Factory, Iran) were engaged to assess the tensile strength of the hydrogels [17]. (N:3).

FT-IR spectrophotometry was used to investigate the functional groups of NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and 100 %. The formation and evolution of new bonds and surface modification of the lyophilized NC gels were assessed by FT-IR spectroscopy (FT-IR Spectrometer, Spectrum 65, USA). (N:3).

The swelling behavior of the NC gels was measured in the PBS solution (pH 7.4), at room temperature. The lyophilized NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % placed into 20 ml PBS and kept at room temperature for 72 h to swell. The samples were drawn from PBS, dried gently, weighted, and replaced into PBS. Equilibrium mass swelling percentages were calculated using the following eq. 2 [30]; (N:3)
(2)
Equilibrium mass swelling
%
=
m 1 – m 0
m 0
× 100
Where m0 and m1 are the dried mass weight and the mass swollen weight of the NC gels, respectively.

The weight loss of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % in PBS (pH 7.4) were used to evaluate the biodegradation rate. The lyophilized NC gels were weighed, and equal-size samples were immersed in PBS (pH 7.4) (15 ml) at 37 C0. At the specific time points 3, 7, 10, and 14 days, the specimens for each percent of Se.DFO-CQDs were taken out from the PBS solution, dried, and weighed. Below eq. (3) was used to calculate the degree of weight loss [30]; (N:3)
(3)
Weight loss
%
=
W 0 – W 1
W 0
× 100
Where W0 is the initial weight of NC gels and W1 is the dry weight after removal from the water.

The liquid displacement method was used to assess the porosity of the lyophilized NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 %, using the following eq. 4; (N:3)
(4)
Porosity
%
=
V 1 – V 3
V 2 – V 3
× 100
Where V1 is the initial volume of 96 % ethanol, V2 is the volume after the dried hydrogels were soaked in (and ethanol filled the pores), and V3 is the volume of ethanol after the dried hydrogels removal.

The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of the NC gels was determined according to the previous studies. Briefly, the opening of a round container with a diameter of 1.5 cm was fixed with the prepared NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 %. The containers were filled with 5 ml of DW and placed in an oven at 37 C0 for 24 h and 48 h. WVTR was calculated using eq. 5; (N:3)
(5)
WVTR
g / m 2 / 24 h & 48
h
=
− ΔW
A
Where ΔW is the water weight (g) change and A is the exposure area (m2). Containers without the hydrogels in their openings were used as the control.

Human blood was engaged to evaluate the blood compatibility of the NC gels. Fresh anticoagulated human blood (2 ml) was added and diluted with PBS (2 ml). The NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % were incubated with 0.3 ml of the diluted blood at 37 C0 for 60 min. Then, the samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant of each sample was transferred to a 96-well plate to determine the absorbance at 545 nm by a microplate reader (Bio Tek synergy, synergy-HTX, USA). The negative control was 0.2 ml diluted blood in 10 ml PBS, and the positive control was 0.2 ml diluted blood in 10 ml deionized water. Eventually, the hemolysis degree was calculated using eq. 6 [30]; (N:3)
(6)
Hemolysis
%
=
Dt – Dnc
Dpc – Dnc
× 100
Where Dt, Dpc, and Dnc are the absorbance of the sample, the positive control, and the negative control, respectively.

Microbial penetration evaluates the permeability of microorganisms. The wound dressing was placed on open 9 ml vials containing 5 ml of Brain heart infusion (BHI) broth (Merck, Germany) (test area: 0.8 cm2). The vials and liquid BHI medium were sterilized by autoclave (121 C0 and 15 psi for 20 min). Then, the NC gels with different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, and 100) were prepared. To sterilize the hydrogels, both sides were placed under UV light for 20 min. After that, 5 mL of liquid BHI medium was poured into 9 glass vials, 7 vials for different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs, and one vial without a cap, as a positive control, and a vial with a cotton ball cap, as a negative control. After adding the medium to the vials, the NC gels were placed on the vials as caps. All the vials were placed at room temperature for 7 days, and on the 3 and 7 days, the absorbance of BHI medium at 600 nm was measured. Also, colony count of grown microorganisms on blood agar and eosin methylene blue (EMB) (Merck, Germany) media was evaluated. (N:3).

The drug release study evaluates the amount of Se.DFO-CQDs, which is released from the NC gels. In this regard, three NC gels with different percentages of Gel/Alg (Gel 7 % / Alg 3 %), (Gel 8 % / Alg 2 %) and (Gel 9 % / Alg 1 %) with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 %, and 100 % were prepared. Then, 1 g piece of each NC gel was cut with dimensions of 10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm using a biopsy punch and then placed into 10 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) and shaken at 37 C0 for 72 h. To measure the released Se.DFO-CQDs into the solution, 500 μL aliquots of the solution were drawn at 30, 60, 120, 240, 480, 720, 1440, 2880, and 4320 min and the amount of Se.DFO-CQDs were measured by spectrophotometer (spectrum lab-53, Iran) at 350 nm (the maximum absorption wavelength for Se.DFO-CQDs). The solution was replenished with 500 μL of PBS at each sampling point, and the concentration was adjusted for the dilution factor. (N:3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant capacity of the NC gels
ce:section.ce:para:
The antioxidant capacity of the NC gels was assessed via the Blois method by DPPH radicals [31]. The NC gels have different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, and 100) and absolute ethanol were used as the samples and the control, respectively. Briefly, 0.3 ml of NC gels were prepared and mixed with 1 ml of 1 mM DPPH, and all samples were incubated at 37 C0 for 13 h, and the absorbance of the samples was measured at 517 nm every hour. The radical scavenging activity was calculated using the following eq. 7: (N:3)
(7)
DPPH scavenging
%
=
Absorbance Control – Absorbance Sample
Absorbance Control
× 100

Human skin fibroblastic (hs-F) cell line was purchased from the National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI), Pasteur Institute of Iran, Tehran, Iran, and cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium: nutrient mixture F-12 (DMEM/F12; Gibco, Grand Island, NY, USA) supplemented with 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS; Gibco, Grand Island, NY, USA), 100 unit/mL of penicillin and 100 mg/mL of streptomycin in a humidified incubator at 37 C0 with CO2 5 %. The cell media was changed every other day.

The cell proliferation on the NC gels was assessed using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl) 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT). The NC gels with different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, and 100) were prepared circularly and put on the bottom of the 96-well plates. The sterilization was performed by UV light for 45 min followed by three times washing with PBS, and once with DMEM/F12. After the sterilization, a density of 5 × 103 cells / well was seeded on the NC gels. At each time point (24 h, 48 h, and 72 h after cells seeding), the culture medium was removed from the 96-well plate and 10 μl of MTT (0.5 mg/ml) was added to each well, and the cells were incubated at 37 C0 for 4 h in a dark place. Then, the solution was removed and 0.1 ml DMSO was added to each well. The absorbance of the samples and control were measured at 570 nm by a microplate reader (Bio Tek, EPOCH, USA) [30]. (N:3)
(8)
Cell viability
%
=
OD
treatment
OD
control
× 100

hs-F cells were seeded at a density of 2 × 104 cells / well in 24-well plates. A scratch was made across the well on 90 %-confluent cell monolayers using a 200 μL sterile micropipette tip, and the wells were washed with Dulbecco's PBS to remove debris and dislodged cells. Then, the cells were treated with the released Se.DFO-CQDs from the NC gels (the NC gel extract) with different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75 and 100). Images of wound closure were taken at 0, 12, and 24 h using an inverted microscope (OPTIKA, Italy) equipped with a digital camera. Image J software (version 1.44 p) obtained the average percentage of the wound area, and in vitro wound closure was calculated using the following eq. 9 [32]. (N:3)
(9)
In vitro wound closure
%
=
Wt 0 – Wti
Wt 0
× 100
Where Wt0 is the wound area on 0 h and Wti is the wound area on 12 h and 24 h.

To evaluate the wound healing ability of the NC gels, a full-thickness excision wound model was created on the back of thirty male Wistar rats (2 months old, weighing 200–220 g), and the wounds were treated with the NC gels and sterile gauze. The animals were purchased from Pasteur Institute (Tehran, Iran) and housed with access to diet and water ad libitum for 3D before experimentation. Animal experiments were approved by the ethical committee of Hamadan University of Medical Sciences and Health Services and were carried out in accordance with the university guidelines (IR.UMSHA.REC.1401.1014). To create the full-thickness excision wound model, the animals were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of Ketamine 100 mg/kg/Xylazine 10 mg/kg (Alfasan, Iran). The dorsal hair of the animals was shaved and disinfected with 70 % ethanol at the operation site, and then a full-thickness wound was made using a scalpel blade in the form of a circle with a diameter of 1.5 cm. The animals were randomly divided into three groups (10 rats per group) as follows: two experimental groups and one control group. The experimental groups were treated with NC gel without Se.DFO-CQDs (0 %) and NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs (50 %). The control group was treated with the sterile gauze. After creating the wound, the NC gels synthesized with a diameter greater than the wound area and were disinfected with UV (for 30 min on both sides) and were placed on the wounds (Figs. S1.c, d, and e). In the first four days, the dressings were changed every 24 h until a fibrin clot formed, and then no dressing was placed on the wounds to allow the natural healing process to take place.

For the wound size evaluation, the macroscopic changes in the wound area were recorded on days 0, 3, 7, 10, and 14 using a camera (Apple Inc., USA), and the wound closure was measured via Image J software (version 1.44 p). The following eq. (10) is used to calculate the wound closure:
(10)
Wound closure
%
=
first
day
wound area − specific wound area
first
day
wound area
× 100

After 7 days, half of the rats in each group (5 rats) and after 14 days the other half (5 rats) were anesthetized, and the margin of the wound tissue that was being repaired was harvested and divided into two parts. One part was immediately placed in liquid nitrogen for molecular evaluations, another part was prepared for histopathological examination. The specimens were fixed in 10 % buffered formalin (pH 7.26), processed and embedded in paraffin. Then, the specimens were cross-sectioned and stained with Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome (MT) stains for visualization under a light microscope. After that, blood samples were taken through the inferior vena cava, and immediately their serum was separated to determine the amount of serum iron by DIRUI biochemistry autoanalyzer (CS-T240Plus, China), and finally, all animals were sacrificed.

The primers for VEGF-A, b-FGF, PDGF-b, COL1A1, lncRNA GAS5, and β-actin (as the housekeeping gene) genes were designed with primer designing tool-NCBI and all primers were blasted on the NCBI primer BLAST. Then, the physical characteristics of the primers were investigated on the website https://www.idtdna.com and the PrimerQuest™ Tool. Finally, the most appropriate primer for each gene selected (Table. S1).

Total RNA was isolated from all tissues by RNX plus solution (Sinaclon, Iran) following the manufacture protocol. The quality and concentration of the extracted RNA were determined through a nanodrop Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The integrity of all extracted RNA was confirmed by 1.2 % agarose gel electrophoresis.

cDNA was synthesized by First Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit (Pars Tous, Iran) using 1 μg total RNA according to the manufacturer's protocol in a final volume of 20 μL. Then, mRNA expression of the genes was investigated using RealQ Plus 2× Master Mix Green (Ampliqon, Denmark) by LightCycler 96 RT-PCR instrument (Roche, Germany) according to kit instructions. Briefly, 5 μL SYBR Green, 0.5 μL forward primer, 0.5 μL reverse primer, 1 μL cDNA, and 3 μL nuclease-free water were added to a tube for each reaction. After 15 min of preincubation of samples at 95 °C, an RT-qPCR was performed in 40 cycles (denaturation: 95 °C for 15 s, annealing: 53 °C to 59 °C for 30 s, extension: 72 °C for 30 s). Product specificity also was confirmed by 2.5 % agarose gel electrophoresis of RT-qPCR products. Expression fold change was calculated using the 2-ΔΔCt formula [33]. (N:2).

Statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 8. 4. 3. (GraphPad Software, Inc.). Statistical differences among the multiple groups were compared using the one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test (normally distributed data) or Kruskal-Wallis test followed by Dunn's multiple comparisons test (nonparametric data). The levels of significance were considered with *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001. Three repetitions were performed for the characterization of Se.DFOs-CQDs, the NC gels, and cell culture studies, and two repetitions were performed for RT-qPCR and their average was reported.

Figs. 1a and b show TEM images and size distribution histograms of the Se.DFO-CQDs, respectively. Fig. 1a depicts TEM images of poly-dispersed Se.DFO-CQDs, which manifests the formation of spherical Se.DFO-CQDs have a size distribution of ~2–14 nm, but most of them have size distribution of ~2–6.

The results of the DLS analysis showed that the DFO-CQDs had an average diameter of 63.9 ± 5.4 nm and a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.468. While Se.DFO-CQDs had an average diameter of 68.7 ± 1.8 nm and a PDI of 0.432 (Figs. 1c and d, respectively). The colloidal stability of Se.DFO-CQDs were assessed by zeta potential analysis. The zeta potential measurement showed that DFO-CQDs and Se.DFO-CQDs had +20.2 ± 0.42 mV and + 15.7 ± 0.24 mV of surface charge (Figs. 1e and f, respectively). Zeta potential results showed an appropriate stability of Se.DFO-CQDs. Generally, it is accepted that for zeta potential values +30 mV to −30 mV, colloidal systems are stable due to the electrostatic repulsions between Se.DFO-CQDs that obviate them from aggregating and collapsing [34].

A FT-IR spectrum shows a molecular fingerprint with each peak indicating to repetitions of vibrations of bonds existent between atoms of the compound. No combination has the identical compound of atoms as the other molecules and compounds. Thus FT-IR spectra of a compound are its unique features and can be engaged for qualitative identification of that combination. FT-IR analysis was carried out to investigate the possible functional groups of Se, DFO, and Se.DFO-CQDs. The FT-IR spectrum for these agents is demonstrated in Fig. 2a. FT-IR spectra of Se showed that several peaks appeared at 415 cm−1 (CI stretch), 665 cm−1 (CCl stretch), 864 cm−1 (CH bend), 1062 cm−1 (CF stretch), 1131 cm−1 (CF stretch), 2295 cm−1 (CH stretch), and 2977 cm−1 (=C-H stretch). Also, FT-IR spectra of DFO displayed that some peaks appeared at 1050 cm−1 (CF stretch), 1195 cm−1 (CF stretch), 1461 cm−1 (NO2 stretch), 1632 cm−1 (CO stretch), 2931 cm−1 (CH stretch), and 3312 cm−1 (OH stretch). Moreover, FT-IR spectra of Se.DFO-CQDs indicated that several peaks appeared at 563 cm−1 (CI stretch), 785 cm−1 (CH bend), 1059 cm−1 (CF stretch), 1207 cm−1 (CF stretch), 1413 cm−1 (NO2 stretch), 1697 cm−1 (CO stretch), and 2945 cm−1 (CH stretch), and 3429 cm−1 (OH stretch).

FT-IR spectra of Se, DFO, and Se.DFO-CQDs showed respectively the presence of a broad peak at 2977 cm−1, 2931 cm−1, and 2945 cm−1, which corresponds to CH group with stretching vibrations. Two sharp peaks were observed at 1632 cm−1 and 1697 cm−1, which belong to DFO and Se.DFO-CQDs, respectively, that corresponds to CO group with stretching vibrations. Moreover, two other sharp peaks were determined at 1461 cm−1 and 1413 cm−1, which are associated with DFO and Se.DFO-CQDs, respectively, that corresponds to NO2 stretch group. FT-IR spectra of Se, DFO, and Se.DFO-CQDs respectively indicated the presence of a broad peak at 1131 cm−1, 1195 cm−1, and 1207 cm−1, which corresponds to CF group with stretching vibrations. Based on the results, FT-IR spectra of Se, DFO, and Se.DFO-CQDs demonstrated the functional groups which remain in the Se.DFO-CQDs after synthesis and doping with Se.

The physical condition of the compound, which is evaluated through XRD, is very important for its release behavior. XRD pattern of Se.DFO-CQD is shown in Fig. 2b. A single sharp diffraction peak around 200 (2θ = 20, (002)) corresponds to an amorphous carbon-based material, which indicates the carbon quantum dots of Se.DFO [35]. Moreover, a short and sharp diffraction peak at 23.90 (2θ = 23.9 (100)) shows the presence of Se in the structure of Se.DFO-CQD indicates the pure hexagonal phase of Se crystals [36]. Also, previous studies have determined that DFO has a crystalline compound with many specific peaks at 19.320, 21.030, 22.560, 23.980, and 28.560, which, in the results of XRD pattern, the presence of a long and sharp diffraction peak around 200 (2θ = 20) indicates DFO in Se.DFO-CQD [37].

The optical properties of Se.DFO-CQD are shown in Figs. 2c and S2. Firstly, a scan was performed using a spectrophotometer from 250 to 500 nm, and the maximum absorption occurred at 350 nm (λmax) (black peak), suggesting the most suitable exciting wavelength for Se.DFO-CQD is 350 nm. Secondly, the sample was irradiated by a spectrofluorometer at a wavelength of 350 nm (red peak) for excitation, then it showed self-emission of fluorescence at 402 nm (blue peak) (Fig. 2c). Additionally, wavelengths of 300 to 390 nm were irradiated to Se.DFO-CQD by the spectrofluorometer and its fluorescence emission were examined, recommending the excitation-independent luminescence feature of Se.DFO-CQD (Fig. S2).

The antioxidant capacity of Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % is shown in Fig. 2d. Se.DFO-CQDs 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % indicated p-value <0.0001 (****p < 0.0001), and Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 20 % and 30 % showed p-value <0.001 (***p < 0.001) compared to the negative control. In the DPPH scavenging assay, the antioxidant capacity and clearance rate were enhanced considerably with the increasing concentration of Se.DFO-CQDs. Se.DFO-CQDs demonstrated potent antioxidant activity with concentrations of 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % (68 ± 1.5 %, 73 ± 1.4 %, and 76 ± 0.9 %, respectively). Also, the clearance rates of DPPH free radical via Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 20 % and 30 % obtained 62 ± 1.5 %, 63 ± 1.3 %, 66 ± 1.3 % respectively. Besides, the antioxidant activity of Se.DFO-CQDs 100 % was investigated at 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210, and 240 min, and the highest amount of antioxidant activity was observed from 150 to 240 min, shown in Fig. S3.

Oxidative stress is an imbalance between antioxidants and oxidants, which results in many diseases [38]. The Fenton Haber-Weiss reaction is one of the most important pathways that generate free radicals. This reaction increases oxidative stress by iron-dependent ROS production, which severely damage macromolecules such as lipids, proteins, RNA, and DNA. To prevent oxidative stress damages, a control of free iron is indispensable [39]. As mentioned above, DFO is a drug approved by the FDA, which chelates iron. Enormous studies have proven that DFO inhibits hydroxyl radical and hydroxyl production by suppressing the Fenton and Haber-Weiss reactions through chelating iron, and eventually reduces oxidative stress [19,40]. Moreover, Se is a trace element that plays a crucial role in numerous antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities through participating in selenoproteins like glutathione peroxidases, thioredoxin reductases, and thioredoxin-glutathione reductase [21]. Based on the results and antioxidant properties of Se.DFO-CQDs, this assay demonstrated that Se.DFO-CQDs significantly decreased oxidative stress due to presenting DFO and Se in the form of CQD.

After the preparation of the NC gel and conversion to gelation form, the SEM analysis of the NC gels with different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs are performed and shown in Fig. 3
(a-g). Observing the morphology of the NC gels, it is figured out that they have a highly porous structure with interconnected pores, which were made via the phase separation during the lyophilization process. This indicates that the polymer concentration of the NC gels played a critical role in the microstructure, such as in the number and size of pores. The pore sizes of all NC gels ranged from 30 to 300 μm and the NC gel with high percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs (75 and 100) had pores of the smallest size and diameter. This phenomenon showed that an enhancement in solid content increased the cross-linking compact and density internal structure. Remarkably, some studies have reported that NC gels matrices with similar microstructures were beneficial for tissue engineering applications, vascularization, and proliferation [34].

The EDX spectra of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 %, and 100 % are illustrated in Fig. 4 (a, b, and c, respectively). The NC gels contain C, Se, and O elements belonging to Se.DFO-CQDs. It is found from EDX spectra, that the concentrations of C, Se, and O elements increased with enhancing Se.DFO-CQDs amount in the NC gels. Moreover, the EDX images of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 %, and 100 % are shown in Figs. S4, S5, and S6, respectively). The EDX mapping demonstrated that Se.DFO-CQDs were well dispersed homogenously in the polymer matrix, and could also confirm the existence of C, Se, and O elements on the surface of the NC gels.

The XRD pattern of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, and 100 % is shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. 5a. The diffractogram of gelatin includes two peaks at 7.750 (small and narrow peak) and 20.080 (broad peak), which are described as helical crystalline structures, and three single crystalline diffraction peaks at 14.30, 21.30, and 37.10 correspond to alginate [41]. As mentioned above, a short and sharp diffraction peak at 23.90 (2θ = 23.9) shows the presence of Se, a long and sharp diffraction peak around 200 (2θ = 20) indicates DFO and a single broad diffraction peak around 240 (2θ = 24) corresponds to amorphous carbon-based materials, which all these factors are related to Se.DFO-CQDs. The results suggest Se, DFO, Se.DFO-CQDs, and the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and 100 % remained in the NC gels after synthesis.

FT-IR analysis was done to investigate the possible functional groups of gelatin, alginate, the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and 100 %, and Se.DFO-CQDs, and demonstrated in Fig. S8 and Fig. 5b. FT-IR spectra of gelatin showed that several peaks appeared at 1060 cm−1 (CF stretch), 1435 cm−1 (CC stretch), 1687 cm−1 (CO stretch), and 3470 cm−1 (OH stretch). Also, FT-IR spectra of alginate displayed that some peaks appeared at 1048 (CF stretch), 1364 cm−1 (NO2 stretch), 1646 cm−1 (CO stretch), and 3298 cm−1 (OH stretch). Moreover, FT-IR spectra of NC gel Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % indicated that several peaks appeared at 1002 cm−1 (CF stretch), 1465 cm−1 (CC stretch), 1554 cm−1 (NO2 stretch), 1688 cm−1 (CO stretch), and 3358 cm−1 (OH stretch). FT-IR spectra of NC gel Se.DFO-CQDs 100 % showed that several peaks appeared at 1020 (CF stretch), 1574 cm−1 (CC stretch), 1690 cm−1 (CO stretch), and 3360 cm−1 (OH stretch). FT-IR spectra of Se.DFO-CQDs was mentioned above.

FT-IR spectra of gelatin, alginate, and the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % showed separately a peak at 1060 cm−1, 1048 cm−1, and 1002 cm−1 corresponds to CF group with stretching vibrations, respectively, three peaks at 1687 cm−1, 1646 cm−1, and 1688 cm−1 belongs to CO stretch group, respectively, and three peaks at 3470 cm−1, 3298 cm−1, and 3358 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretch group, respectively (Fig. S8). On the other hand, FT-IR spectra of the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 100 %, and Se.DFO-CQDs indicated separately a peak at 1002 cm−1, 1020 cm−1, and 1059 cm−1 corresponds to CF group with stretching vibrations, respectively, three peaks at 1688 cm−1, 1690 cm−1, and 1697 cm−1 belongs to CO stretch group respectively, and three broad peaks at 3358 cm−1, 3360 cm−1, and 3429 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretch group, respectively ( Fig. 5b). According to the FT-IR results, common peaks among gelatin, alginate, and the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and 100 %, and Se.DFO-CQDs demonstrated the functional groups that remained in these factors after the synthesis of the NC gel, suggesting that the synthesis was appropriately carried out.

The stress–strain curves of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 % and 100 % are demonstrated in Fig. 5c. Based on the results, tensile strains were obtained 81 ± 1.99 %, 75 ± 1.35 %, and 67 ± 1.28 %, and tensile stress were obtained 65 ± 2.32 kPa, 79 ± 2.56 kPa, and 88 ± 2.98 kPa for the NC gels (Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 % and 100 % respectively). More importantly, with increasing Se.DFO-CQDs, the tensile strain property decreased, and on the contrary, the tensile stress property increased. Accordingly, with the increase of Se.DFO-CQDs, the tensile strength of the NC gels enhanced, which causes more force to tear the NC gel. The elasticity of the NC gels increases resilience to burst pressure and mechanical support in the wound area. According to previous studies, the strong adhesive features of NC gels can be attributed to hydrogen bonding in the hydrogel matrix, covalent bonding during crosslinking, and hydrogel-tissue interlocking [30].

Swelling or water uptake assessment shows the water absorption by the NC gels. This test was carried out by the water displacement method. Fig. 5 d shows the 72 h time course of the swelling of the NC gel swelling with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % in PBS (pH 7.4), at ambient temperature. The mass swelling ratio of NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % were obtained 130 ± 4.3 % (1500 min), 115 ± 4.6 % (900 min), 146 ± 4.1 % (1800 min), 168 ± 4 % (2100 min), 146 ± 4.1 % (360 min), 143 ± 4.6 % (600 min), and 154 ± 6.8 % (900 min) respectively. After the NC gels reached swelling equilibrium, the swelling percentage of NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, and 30 % remained constant, while the swelling percentage of NC gels with Se remained constant. DFO-CQDs decreased by 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % unremarkably.

The swelling behavior of NC gels is influenced by the free energy changes through mobile ions, the network elasticity, and the mixing of polymers and ions [42]. The NC gels revealed vivid swelling features in the early hours, which means this wound dressing is valuable and beneficial for wound healing because it could absorb excessive wound exudates, which would improve wound repairing by promoting keratinocyte migration and fibroblast proliferation [43].

Weight loss assessment was performed to measure the biodegradation of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % in PBS (pH 7.4). As shown in Fig. 6 a, statistical analysis indicated that all NC gels' biodegradation was <75 % during 14 days. The weight loss analysis showed that the biodegradation of all prepared NC gels was in the acceptable range of 18 % to 75 % during 14 days. In other words, the highest weight loss and biodegradation was observed in NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, which means almost 75 % of the initial weight has been lost after 14 days. On the other, the least biodegradation was perceived in the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 100 %, which means approximately 50 % of its initial weight has been lost after 14 days. More importantly, a significant difference was observed in the biodegradation percentage of the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % compared to 100 % on day 3 (*p < 0.05), day 7 (*p < 0.05), day 10 (*p < 0.05) and day 14 (**p < 0.01), compared to the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 75 % on day 10 (*p < 0.05) and day 14 (**p < 0.01), and compared to the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % on day 14 (*p < 0.05).

The biodegradation rate of NC gels is a crucial factor that determines the efficiency of the treatment during skin repair. This parameter has a direct relation with releasing Se.DFO-CQDs from the matrix of wound dressing. Our results demonstrated that the biodegradation rate of the NC gels is in the admissible range of 18 to 75 %. It is found that the high concentration of Se.DFO-CQDs decreased the biodegradation rate. Several factors can affect the NC gel biodegradation, including the hydrophilicity behavior of polymers, the density of polymers, the number and position of linkage points susceptible to hydrolysis, and the molecular weight of the polymers [44].

The porosity assessment of scaffolds, as a wound dressing, is a crucial parameter that affects the absorption of exudates from the wound site and decreases the probability of infection [45]. The porosity of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % were evaluated using the liquid displacement method. Based on the results, the porosity of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % was obtained 75 ± 2.5 %, 77 ± 2.8 %, 79 ± 3.1 %, 82 ± 3.6 %, 73 ± 3.2 %, 69 ± 3.6 %, and 68 ± 3.5 % respectively. Importantly, as shown in Fig. 6b, the porosity of all prepared NC gels is high enough (>67 %) and suitable for wound dressing applications [46]. The NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 30 % demonstrated the highest porosity among all prepared NC gels. However, there was no statistically significant difference between the porosity percentages of all NC gels.

WVTR is closely related to the wound healing process, and an appropriate wound dressing should be able to control the gas exchange. In this study, WVTR was carried out through the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 %. The results suggested that the lowest WVTR after 24 h (1879 g/m2/24 h) and 48 (1997 g/m2/48 h) corresponded to the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, and the highest WVTR after 24 h (2357 g/m2/24 h) and 48 h (2484 g/m2/48 h) corresponded to the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 100 %. Although increasing Se.DFO-CQDs percentage resulted in the WVTR enhanced, no significant difference was observed between the NC gels with different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs. On the other hand, the WVTR of all NC gels showed the same significant difference compared to the control group (OPC). (***p < 0.001) ( Fig. 6c).

WVTR for the granulating wound, skin with first-degree burn, and normal skin was expressed as 5000, 300, and 200 g/m2/24 h, respectively [47]. The high WVTR results in scar formation through dehydration of the wound area, and the low WVTR leads to the accumulation of excessive exudates and augments the risk of bacterial infection, eventually postponing the wound-repairing process. Thus, an ideal wound dressing should have a WVTR of 2000–2500 g/m2/24 h, inhibiting dryness and dehydration and preventing the exudate formation of the wound site [48]. Our results state that WVTR of the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % were 2000–2500 (g/m2/24 & 48 h), suggesting that the prepared NC gels had suitable WVTR properties for wound dressing.

Blood compatibility was performed to evaluate the hemolysis, which is caused by the NC gels. As shown in Fig. 6d, the hemolysis rate of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % was lower than the positive control, and different percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs showed a significant difference with the positive control (****p < 0.0001), which indicates the appropriate blood compatibility of prepared NC gels. Also, with the increase in the percentage of Se.DFO-CQDs, the amount of hemolysis decreased, but no significant difference was observed between the percentages of Se.DFO-CQDs.

The interaction between the blood component, especially erythrocytes, and the combination of biomaterials is important in assigning the biomaterials' fate and the remedy's success. Hemolysis is described as the hemoglobin released into plasma due to erythrocyte damage. It is also a critical parameter for biomaterials used in tissue engineering and wound healing. Wang et al. have proven that hemolysis is straightly associated with the blood compatibility of biomaterials [49]. The blood compatibility results stated that the hemolysis of the NC gels was considerably less than the positive control, notably, the hemocompatibility of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % (6.9 ± 0.1 %), 75 % (5 ± 0.9 %), and 100 % (4.1 ± 0.7 %) were lower than 7 %, suggesting these NC gels were strongly non-hemolytic, and the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, and 30 % had hemolysis ratio of 10 ± 0.6 %, 9.5 ± 0.5 %, 8.9 ± 0.4 %, and 8 ± 0.1 %, respectively. Additionally, Sun et al. have proven that DFO has an antioxidant activity by chelating iron and suppressing the Fenton reaction, ultimately preventing damage to the erythrocytes' membrane and hemolysis [50]. In addition to DFO in the structure of Se.DFO-CQDs, Se also have antioxidant effects due to its presence in glutathione peroxidases, thioredoxin reductases, and thioredoxin-glutathione reductase, and protects the membrane of erythrocytes [21].

To evaluate Se.DFO-CQDs release three types of NC gels (Gel 7 % / Alg 3 %, Gel 8 % / Alg 2 %, and Gel 9 % / Alg 1 %) with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 50 % and 100 % were synthesized, and Se.DFO-CQDs quantity was measured during 72 h. The amount of Se.DFO-CQDs from Gel 7 % / Alg 3 %, Gel 8 % / Alg 2 %, and Gel 9 % / Alg 1 % reached the maximum at 4, 12, and 24 h after exposure to PBS (Figs. S9, S10, and S11, respectively). In addition, Fig. 7a shows the release rate of Se.DFO-CQDs was 100 % from three types of the NC gels. The results suggested that Se.DFO-CQDs were released so rapidly from the NC gel with Gel 7 % / Alg 3 % and reached maximum at first 4 h due to low concentration of gelatin in the NC gel structure. Also, the release pattern of Se.DFO-CQDs from the NC gel with Gel 9 % / Alg 1 % were very slow and reached a maximum at 24 h because the gelatin concentration was high. On the other hand, the release of Se.DFO-CQDs from the NC gel with Gel 8 % / Alg 2 % reached a maximum at 12 h in a controlled manner to be effective because the release depends on the concentration of the polymers, especially gelatin, suggesting the NC gel with Gel 8 % / Alg 2 % was appropriate and selected for the next steps of the study.

The ability of a wound dressing to prohibit the penetration of microorganisms is very important to prevent infection. Microbial penetration assessment of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % was done on day 3 and 7. Absorbance and colony count of microbial contamination were investigated in all vials containing BHI broth medium covered with the NC gel caps. As shown in Fig. 7b, the absorbance of BHI broth medium with the NC gel caps is significantly lower than the positive control (vial without cap) on day 3 and 7 (the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, and 75 %, had **p < 0.01 on day 3 and 7, and the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 100 % had ***p < 0.001 on day 3 and 7). In addition, colony count was investigated on days 3 and 7. On the third day, the sample from the positive control vial caused the growth of microorganisms on the blood agar medium. On the seventh day, in addition to the growth of microorganisms on the blood and EMB agar medium, the sample from the vial caped by the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % also caused the growth of few colonies on the blood agar medium Figs. S12-S15.

The microbial penetration results through the NC gels showed that this wound dressing can be used for skin repairing due to the presence of DFO and Se in the structure of Se.DFO-CQDs, and also gelatin and alginate in the NC gels. Based on the previous studies, DFO can suppress the growth of bacterial species through disrupting iron-dependent metabolic pathways [51]. Also, numerous research have reported that Se can have antibacterial and antifungal features owing to some mechanisms including; photocatalytic property against many microorganisms, prohibiting of the microorganism attachment to the surface and biofilm formation, disrupting the activity of some enzymes such as the dehydrogenase in order to the destruction of the cell membrane integrity [52]. Additionally, many studies have stated that the combination of gelatin and alginate can be used in tissue engineering and wound healing due to antibacterial property [53,54]. Due to these reasons, the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs has a great antimicrobial feature to imply for wound dressing and protect against secondary microbial infection at the wound area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant capacity of the NC gels
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant capacity of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % are demonstrated in Fig. 7c. According to the results, the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 75 % and 100 % showed p-value <0.0001 (****p < 0.0001), with Se.DFO-CQDs 20 %, 30 %, and 50 % demonstrated p-value <0.001 (***p < 0.001), with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 % displayed p-value <0.01 (**p < 0.01), and with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % showed p-value <0.05 (*p < 0.05) compared to the negative control. Besides, the antioxidant activity of Se.DFO-CQDs 100 % was investigated at 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210, and 240 min, and the highest amount of antioxidant activity was observed of 480 to 780 min ( Fig. 7d).

As mentioned before, to prevent oxidative stress damages at wound site, a control of free radicals is necessary. DFO and Se can have antioxidant properties through their own metabolic pathways that were mentioned earlier (DFO interrupts the Fenton Haber-Weiss reactions and Se is present in antioxidant enzymes) [21,40]. Furthermore, many experiments have reported that the combination of gelatin and alginate can be used for wound repairing. For instance, Lihao et al. suggested that a novel hydrogel scaffold might be effective to manage diabetic wound healing via salvianolic acid B‑sodium alginate/gelatin [55]. Also, Cheng et al. have shown that the gelatin/alginate-based macroporous hydrogel effectively suppresses intracellular ROS and accelerates wound healing [56]. Altogether, our results showed that the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs considerably suppress and restrain oxidative stress owing to the existence of DFO and Se in the structure of Se.DFO-CQDs, and also natural polymers like gelatin and alginate in the structure of hydrogels. The quantitative comparison of the obtained characterization results are presented in Tables S2–7.

MTT assay was carried out to determine the percentage of cell viability. As shown in Fig. 8a, at the first 24 h, the cell viability of hs-F increased significantly by the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 30 %, and 50 % (*p < 0.05 and ****p < 0.0001), but Se.DFO-CQDs 75 % and 100 % considerably reduced the cell viability (**p < 0.01 and ****p < 0.0001, respectively). Also, after 48 h, the cell viability of hs-F increased considerably by the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, and 50 % (*p < 0.05, *p < 0.05, ****p < 0.0001, and ****p < 0.0001, respectively), but Se.DFO-CQDs 75 %, and 100 % demonstrated a significant reduction in the cell viability (****p < 0.0001). In addition, after 72 h, the percentage of cell viability was enhanced remarkably by the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, and 50 % (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001, and ****p < 0.0001, respectively), but Se.DFO-CQDs 75 %, and 100 % displayed a considerable decreasing effect on the cell viability (****p < 0.0001).

The suitable cytocompatibility of the NC gels is required for medical applications. For this purpose, the cell viability of the NC gels was evaluated by MTT assay. Our results showed that all prepared NC gels, as well as the control had no toxicity within 24 h to 72 h when in contact with hs-F. After 24, 48, and 72 h, the cell viability of the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs were in the range of 74 %–130 %, 68 %–137 %, and 52 %–139 %, respectively (compared to the control as 100 %). Importantly, the lowest and highest cell viability belong to the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 100 % and 50 % respectively. Based on the MTT results, the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % had the most appropriate effect on cell viability and suggest the supporting of hs-F proliferation due to its cytocompatible nature. The results are in agreement with several previous studies; for example, Jeckson et al. created and reported a wound dressing nanofiber with DFO that activated the proliferation of human dermal fibroblast (HDF) cell lines and was so effective for the treatment of diabetic foot ulcer [24]. Moreover, Gao et al. made a chitosan/gelatin hydrogel with DFO and hydroxysafflor yellow A, as a wound dressing and demonstrated that this combination could stimulate human epithelial keratinocytes proliferation and be suitable for diabetic wound healing [57].

Scratch test is a low-cost and easy method to investigate in vitro wound healing through cell migration and proliferation. This technique provides an environment that imitates the wound-healing procedure in vivo [24]. As shown in Figs. 8b and c, at the first 12 h, the wound closure rate increased significantly by the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, and 50 % (****p < 0.0001), but the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 75 %, and 100 % had a negative effect on the wound closure, which was statistically significant on the cell migration (****p < 0.0001). Moreover, after 24 h, the wound closure by the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 20 %, 30 %, and 50 % enhanced remarkably (****p < 0.0001), but the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 75 %, and 100 % had a decreasing effect on the wound closure and cell migration, which was statistically considerable (****p < 0.0001). It is noteworthy that the average of wound closure by the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, and 10 % increased after 24 h, but it was not statistically significant.

Fig. 8c demonstrates the cell migration and proliferation to the wound area over 24 h in the presence of the NC gel extraction and DMEM medium as control. The NC gel extraction was employed in this method because the NC gel mat was found to obstruct the scratch area observation. The released Se.DFO-CQDs from the NC gel would attribute the potential healing impact that could be delivered to the wound site. Fig. 8c depicts the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % appropriately stimulated cell migration and proliferation, and covered most of the scratch site. Also, it was found that the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % was more effective than the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, and control. On the other hand, the released Se.DFO-CQDs from the NC gels 75 % and 100 % did not activate cell migration and proliferation at the wound area. After 12 h, the average wound closure was 78.2 ± 5.2 % for the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 %, and 11 ± 2.5 % for the control, and after 24 h, the average of wound closure was 88 ± 5.3 % for the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 %, and 50 ± 3.4 % for the control. The released Se.DFO-CQDs from the NC gels 50 % was able to close the scratch area in 12 and 24 h, which is more about 67 % and 50 % faster than the control. The findings agree with some previous studies, for instance, Jeckson et al. stated that the released DFO from the bilayer nanofiber, as a wound dressing, stimulated the migration of HDF cell lines and had suitable potential therapeutic effect on diabetic foot ulcers [24]. Generally, the results of MTT and in vitro wound healing studies indicated incorporating Se.DFO-CQDs with a concentration of 50 % in NC gels led to these prepared hydrogels being more appropriate for cell migration and proliferation. They implied a considerable potential to enhance wound closure efficiency with healing and therapeutic ability.

The therapeutic and healing effects of the NC gels were further investigated through an in vivo study on the rat animal model, and the results are presented in Fig. 9
. As shown in Fig. 9a, there are no signs of infection or inflammation in any of the experimental groups. Fig. 9a demonstrates the macroscopic changes in the wound healing process that the wound margin in the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % group is unclear after 14 days, which approves the regeneration of the wound margin in this group. In addition, on the 7th day, the wound healing process in the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % group has progressed better and more effectively compared to the control group and the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % group.

In order to quantify the wound healing process, the wound closure percent was measured ( Fig. 9b). On the 3th, 7th, 10th, and 14th days, the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % group possessed higher average of wound closure percent compared to the control group and the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % group (****p < 0.0001, and # # # # p < 0.0001, respectively). Besides, on the 3th, and 7th days, the percentage of wound closure in the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % group demonstrated a significant difference compared to the control group (****p < 0.01, and **p < 0.0001, respectively), while no considerable difference was observed on the 10th, and 14th days. The average wound closure for the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % group on the 3th, 7th, 10th, and 14th days were 57.9 ± 2.65 %, 85.7 ± 3.98 %, 96.4 ± 4.26 %, and 98.1 ± 3.95 % respectively, for the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % group on the 3th, 7th, 10th, and 14th days were 46.4 ± 1.33 %, 56.3 ± 1.54 %, 62.9 ± 2.23 %, and 72 ± 3.48 % respectively, for the control group on the 3th, 7th, 10th, and 14th days were 8.4 ± 0.56 %, 48 ± 2.35 %, 59.5 ± 2.69 %, and 68.6 ± 3.75 %, respectively.

The efficacy of NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 %, as a wound dressing, was examined by the in vivo study. The in vivo findings revealed that the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs had higher wound closure than the control group (sterile gauze-treated wound); notably, the NC gel with Se achieved the highest percentage of wound closure.DFO-CQDs 50 %. According to the macroscopic changes and wound closure quantification results, it can be concluded that the novel NC gel dressing, especially with the concentration of Se.DFO-CQDs 50 %, can incredibly improve the wound healing and skin regeneration. Similarly, Zhang et al. made an injectable bioactive dressing based on platelet-rich plasma and nanoclay containing DFO and proved that this wound dressing could accelerate wound repairing by activating angiogenesis and macrophage polarization in normal rats and type I diabetic rats [58]. Moreover, Nour et al. synthesized biomimetic hybrid scaffold containing niosomal DFO and indicated that this scaffold can be effective in novel vessel formation, early angiogenesis, re-epithelialization, and faster wound healing process [59].

After taking blood from the inferior vena cava, the amount of serum iron was determined by a biochemistry autoanalyzer. As depicted in Fig. 9c, there was no significant difference in serum iron levels in all groups on days 7 and 14, which proves that Se.DFO-CQDs had a local effect and did not enter the bloodstream to bind to iron and reduce its serum level.

The survival of cells like fibroblasts and keratinocytes, neoformed granulation, and tissue regeneration depend on angiogenesis and neovascularization, which provide oxygen and metabolites to the organs and tissues [60]. To assess the wound repairing process from the histological skin specimen of three groups on days 7th and 14th were evaluated by H&E and MT staining (Fig. 10 ), which stained the fibroblast cells in purple, collagen fibers in pale pink for H&E staining, on the other, red blood cells in red and collagen in grey-blue for MT staining. The results showed that the infiltration of the inflammatory cells is evident and the epidermal layer did not form in the control group. On the other hand, the wound treated with the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % exhibited the highest resemblance to normal skin (complete formation of epidermal layer, reduced infiltration of the inflammatory cells, presence of hair follicles and sebaceous glands). The MT staining revealed that the highest collagen synthesis, maturation, and orientation was observed in the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 %, while the collagen deposition and maturation are lowest in the control group. Although the pure hydrogel enhanced the healing process, loading of Se.DFO-CQDs significantly enhanced the healing efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The NC gel with se.DFO-CQDs 50 % increased the expression of VEGF-A
ce:section.ce:para: Relative expression of VEGF-A, bFGF, PDGF-b, COL1A1, and lncRNA GAS5 genes was evaluated 7 and 14 days after wound creation to indicate angiogenesis, cell migration, proliferation, and remodeling potential of the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and 50 % compared to control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The NC gel with se.DFO-CQDs 50 % upregulated the expression of bFGF
ce:section.ce:para: RT-qPCR results showed a significant increase in mRNA expression of bFGF by the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % compared to the control group (*p < 0.05), however, there was no considerable difference between the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and 50 %, after 7 days. On the other hand, after 14 days, no significant difference was observed in the expression level of bFGF among all groups ( Fig. 11b). It could be because homeostasis and releasing of growth factors like bFGF often occur in the initial phases of skin repair.

According to the results, Se.DFO-CQDs could significantly increase the mRNA expression of bFGF in the early days and has an effect on accelerating wound healing. In agreement with our results, several studies have shown that bFGF can significantly promote wound repair by inducing neovascularization, angiogenesis, and mitogenic features [66]. Comparably, Kushibiki et al. designed and synthesized a photocrosslinked gelatin hydrogel and showed this wound dressing improves skin repairing through the release of bFGF and cell proliferation [67]. Similarly, Bahadoran et al. developed a polyvinyl alcohol/sodium alginate hydrogel that incorporated bFGF and reported this scaffold can accelerate wound healing via stimulating fibroblast migration and proliferation [68]. In addition, Huang et al. designed and constructed hydrogel-encapsulated black phosphorus quantum dots as a wound dressing and stated that these quantum dots in the hydrogel can accelerate wound repairing in diabetic rats through upregulating bFGF and VEGF, and also reducing the inflammatory response [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The NC gel with se.DFO-CQDs 50 % enhanced the expression of PDGF-b
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in ( Fig. 11c), a remarkable increase at mRNA expression of PDGF-b was observed by the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % in comparison to control group and the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % (*p < 0.05) during the first 7 days, however, there was no significant difference between the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and control group. After 14 days, the expression of PDGF-b was enhanced by the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % but no considerable difference was observed among all groups. Like numerous other growth factors, PDGF-b has the greatest effect in the initial phases such as homeostasis, angiogenesis, and proliferation after creating wound [61].

Our results demonstrated that Se.DFO-CQDs remarkably increase the mRNA expression of PDGF-b in the early days and has an effect on promoting skin repairing. In agree with the results, many investigation have proven that PDGF-b can significantly accelerate wound healing through proliferation and migration of fibroblast cells, and the recruitment of inflammatory cells [7]. Jian et al. constructed a PDGF-b-derived supramolecular hydrogel and expressed this scaffold can stimulate neovascularization, angiogenesis, collagen deposition, and skin regeneration via PDGF-b [7]. Also, Zhang et al. designed a PDGF-bb/sodium alginate/dextran injectable hydrogel as a wound dressing and showed this hydrogel could accelerate bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs)-mediated wound repairing through promoting angiogenesis [70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The NC gel with se.DFO-CQDs 50 % increased the expression of COL1A1
ce:section.ce:para: The results showed no significant difference in the mRNA expression of COL1A1 among all groups after 7 days. In contrast, there was a remarkable difference between the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % and control group after 14 days (**p < 0.01). Also, the expression of COL1A1 was enhanced by the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % compared to the control group, but this overexpression wasn't significant ( Fig. 11d). As mentioned, COL1A1 is one of the most important factors that participate in the remodeling phase of wound healing, which happens in the late days after injury, and it is expected that this crucial factor would increase at this time and cause re-epithelialization and remodeling to contract the wound area [8].

Our results demonstrated that Se.DFO-CQDs significantly caused the upregulation of COL1A1 in the late days after wound creation, which promotes re-epithelialization and remodeling to accelerate skin regeneration. In agreement with our findings, Shadab et al. designed a ginseng/sodium alginate hydrogel that improves wound healing through increasing COL1A1, COL4A1, VEGF, TGF β1, and EGF [71]. Similarly, Butenko et al. developed a gelatin-based hydrogel as a wound dressing that influences immune and stromal cells to improve wound repair. Their findings proved that this hydrogel can modulate macrophages and fibroblasts, and also be effective on remodeling during wound healing through overexpression of COL1A1 [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The NC gel with se.DFO-CQDs 50 % caused the overexpression of lncRNA GAS5
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in ( Fig. 11e), a significant enhancement at mRNA expression of lncRNA GAS5 was observed via the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % compared to control group and the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % after 7 days (*p < 0.05), however, there was no remarkable difference between the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 0 % and control group. Also, after 14 days, the expression of lncRNA GAS5 was slightly increased by the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs 50 % but no considerable difference was observed among all groups.

Our results demonstrated that the NC gel with Se.DFO-CQDs could considerably increase the mRNA expression of lncRNA GAS5 in the early days and have an effect on skin repair through activating angiogenesis. In agreement with our results, several studies have shown that lncRNA GAS5 can significantly accelerate wound healing through inducing neovascularization and angiogenesis. For example, Peng et al. showed that lncRNA GAS5 can activate the HIF1-α/VEGF pathway and stimulate angiogenesis through binding to TAF15 to accelerate wound healing in diabetic foot ulcers [10]. Moreover, You He et al. investigated lncRNA GAS5/miR-217/Prox1 axis and stated that lncRNA GAS5 sponged miR-217 to increase the expression of Prox1, induce lymphangiogenesis, and eventually promote diabetic wound repairing [9].

In conclusion, we designed, fabricated, and characterized gelatin/alginate-based hydrogels incorporating Se.DFO-CQDs as a wound dressing. Initially, Se.DFO-CQDs were synthesized and characterized using several tests, including TEM, FT-IR, XRD, fluorimetry, UV–Vis spectra, DLS, zeta potential, and DPPH radical scavenging. Based on the results, Se.DFO-CQDs were appropriately designed and synthesized, specifically showing great antioxidant activity. Next, the NC gels were fabricated through an ionic crosslinking approach and investigated using characterization tests such as SEM, EDX, FT-IR, XRD, tensile strength, water uptake, WVTR, weight loss, porosity, bloodcompatibility, microbial penetration, and DPPH. According to the results, the NC gels had good properties for wound dressing applications, especially in radical scavenging, inhibiting microbial penetration, and bloodcompatibility. Following this, the NC gels with Se.DFOCQDs were evaluated by in vitro tests. The findings indicated that the NC gels with Se.DFOCQDs had significant effects on cell viability and proliferation. Subsequently, in vivo studies demonstrated that the NC gels incorporating Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % and without Se.DFO-CQDs had higher wound closure percentages compared to the control group. Additionally, histological evaluations showed that the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % promoted greater neovascularization, re-epithelialization, and collagen synthesis. Moreover, RT-qPCR results indicated that the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs at 50 % considerably upregulated the mRNA expression of VEGF-A, bFGF, PDGF-b, and lncRNA GAS5 on the 7th day, and COL1A1 on the 14th day. Based on the findings, it can be concluded that the NC gels with Se.DFO-CQDs offer a novel approach for the design of wound dressings, and have great potential for wound repair in future clinical applications. However, further studies are needed to achieve therapeutic goals in the clinic.

In wound healing studies, along with RT-qPCR in the molecular section, immunohistochemistry, western blotting, and ELISA approaches can be used to provide a more comprehensive view of the factors involved in wound healing. Also, in addition to studding 7 and 14 days for molecular and histopathological analysis, exploring 3, 10, and 21 days can also be beneficial due to tissue changes and the high variability in the expression of different genes involved in the wound healing process. Furthermore, aside from nanocomposite hydrogel, the effects of nanofibrous dressings and injectable hydrogels containing Se.DFO-CQD on the wound healing process can also be evaluated to determine the most effective wound dressing incorporating Se.DFO-CQD.
